title: Angular Constraint Embedding via SpherePair Loss for Constrained Clustering
abstract:
Constrained clustering integrates domain knowledge through pairwise constraints.
However, existing deep constrained clustering (DCC) methods are either limited by anchors inherent in end-to-end modeling or struggle with learning discriminative Euclidean embedding, restricting their scalability and real-world applicability.
To avoid their respective pitfalls, we propose a novel angular constraint embedding approach for DCC, termed SpherePair.
Using the SpherePair loss with a geometric formulation,
our method faithfully encodes pairwise constraints and leads to embeddings that are clustering-friendly in angular space, effectively separating representation learning from clustering. 
SpherePair preserves pairwise relations without conflict, 
removes the need to specify the exact number of clusters, 
generalizes to unseen data, 
enables rapid inference of the number of clusters, 
and is supported by rigorous theoretical guarantees.
Comparative evaluations with state-of-the-art DCC methods on diverse benchmarks, along with empirical validation of theoretical insights, confirm its superior performance, scalability, and overall real-world effectiveness.
Code is available at [our repository](https://github.com/spherepaircc/SpherePairCC/tree/main).

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper proposes SpherePair, a novel angular constraint embedding method for deep constrained clustering (DCC) that encodes pairwise constraints in angular space via a geometric loss. SpherePair overcomes limitations of prior methods by enabling scalable, clustering-friendly embeddings without requiring the exact number of clusters, and demonstrates superior performance and generalization with theoretical and empirical support.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
**Strengths** 

**1.** This work addresses the deep constrained clustering (DCC) using angular constraint embedding learning without anchors.

**2.** Theoretical foundation is established.

**3.** Sphere pair design explicitly removes the reliance on Euclidean distance and anchors. 


**Weaknesses**

**1.** The authors claim that SpherePair does not require the exact number of clusters K during embedding learning, due to removing the anchors (Line 10). This feature is deemed as the conceptual difference compared with previous end-to-end DCC methods (Line 86). However, in practice, the SpherePair still relies on K, for example, y_i in Equation (1) is essentially determined by K; in Theorem 4.3., the value of ω is related to K. In other words, the K is implicitly introduced to the proposed method. This is computationally nontrivial and contradicts, to some extent, the original motivation of eliminating dependency on K. 

**2.** The article provides theoretical guidance for the selection of hyperparameters ω and D (Theorem 4.3), and the derivation of this theory depends on the total number of categories K. However, in practical applications, the author emphasizes that one of the advantages of his method is that it does not require an exact number of clusters, and recommends directly setting ω=2 and a sufficiently large D, which contradicts Theorem 4.3 in the author's article. Moreover, the author fails to fully demonstrate why the heuristic choice of ω=2 is still universal and optimal when K is unknown, nor does he provide more quantitative guidance on sufficiently large D or discuss the possible overfitting risks it may bring.

**3.**  All derivations assume Lang =0 (i.e., a perfect embedding), which is rarely attainable in practice; the paper lacks any relaxed analysis or error‐bound estimation for the case Lang > 0.

QUESTIONS:
See the Weaknesses above.

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper proposed a novel angular constraint embedding approach for deep constrained clustering, termed SpherePair. The proposed method encodes pairwise constraints and leads to embeddings that are clustering-friendly in angular space.  Comparative evaluations with state-of-the-art DCC methods on diverse benchmarks, along with empirical validation of theoretical insights.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths 
1, this paper proposed a novel angular constraint embedding approach for deep constrained clustering.
2, The proposed method encodes pairwise constraints and leads to embeddings that are clustering-friendly in angular space.
3, A lot of theoretical results are provided.
4, The experiments are well organized.
Weaknesses
1, The motivation of using sphere is not clear, which is considered as the main contribution of this paper.
2, Based on the sphere, the loss is constructed in equation 1. The main contribution of proposing a loss is a little simple.
3, The SpherePair CC algorithm should be described in detail.

QUESTIONS:
See the weakness.

LIMITATIONS:
N/A
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The paper deals with constrained clustering, a "weakly" supervised clustering paradigm that uses pairwise supervision to guide the clustering of data. The authors propose a new deep constraint embedding approach they term SpherePair. The authors state that its distinguishing feature compared to prior work on deep constraint embedding methods is that it operates on cosine similarity instead of Euclidean distances. The authors conduct experiments on 6 simple datasets where the proposed method appears to outperform baselines in most settings.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
## Strengths
- The paper is well structured and easy to follow
- Experiments are conducted on a decent number of datasets and with good baseline comparisons.
- Experimental setup and details are well explained
	
## Neutral
- The authors provide a theoretical results which can inform the choice of the angular scaling parameter ω and the embedding dimension D. I'm not convinced that this part requires an entire page of the paper since it's not like it removes a significant amount of the hyper parameters that have to be set/tuned like λ, the margin parameter pi, and other hyper parameters of the encoder and decoder networks. 

 ## Weaknesses
- The contributions of the work are either very limited, or not well laid out/explained by the authors. As I understand, the methodological contribution compared to other deep constrained clustering approaches is simply to adapt a deep constraint embedding method so that it uses a logistic loss with a cosine similarity and a margin parameter.  This loss function is, in my eyes, only a small contribution to the field as similar loss functions have been seen countless times in related contrastive representation learning literature. 
- All datasets are quite small in the number of samples. If this is a limitation of the proposed method or the paradigm, it should be discussed. 
- I would like to see standard deviations for Table 1. I know recreating such a large table is a massive amount of work, so having them for some of the results in the appendix would be sufficient. Right now, I have no way to tell how significant the "improvements" in performance are compared to the baselines. 
- All datasets that are used are generally considered "easy", such as MNIST, imagenet10. This is not a big weakness, but it makes the work seem less interesting. How would it perform in the real world?

QUESTIONS:
- One of the main contributions appears to be that the proposed method operates on cosine similarity rather than Euclidean distance. However, when one applies Euclidean distance to l2 normalized vectors, the Euclidean distance and cosine distance are the same up to a factor: euclidean_distance(u,v) = sqrt(2-2*cos(u, v)) . Thus clustering of normalized vectors using euclidean distance generally results in the same partitioning as clustering with cosine distance. This makes the contribution of the current paper seem trivial. Thus, 
  - (1) as a simple baseline, I would like to see  how related deep constraint embedding methods that use Euclidean distance perform when the embeddings are simply l2 normalized, after the encoding step. 
  - (2) I would like the authors to argue why their method is still needed if one can just normalize the embeddings to achieve clustering via cosine similarity/distance. 
- Why are all the chosen datasets small? Can the method scale to a million or more samples?
- What was the computational setup? How much gpu memory is required to run these experiments, how long did the experiments take? 
- What is the complexity of the method? How does it scale with the number of dimensions, number of samples, number of constraints?

LIMITATIONS:
yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The work proposes a deep constrained clustering approach that incorporates  an angular loss called SpherePair loss to improve the cluster separation, leading to improved performance in clustering tasks. The method is inspired from deep angular learning from supervised classification literature and is combined with clustering at the embedding space. Real data experiments are presented to showcase the effectiveness of the approach.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:

1.	The paper is overall well-written and organized, and therefore easy to follow.

2.	The literature study and comparing and contrasting different lines of clustering approaches are also presented well.

3.	Theoretical analysis is also a plus with deriving some key hyperparameter values based on it. 

Weaknesses:

1.	Performance improvement is not significant in most of the clustering tasks.

2.	The advantage of embedding-based clustering over end-to-end DCC is not substantiated well.

QUESTIONS:
Questions.

1.	The proposed SpherePair loss has close similarity with the binary cross entropy loss used in many end-to-end DCC works. There is no discussion related to that and the advantages of this loss over them.

2.	How is angle is calculated in the loss function in (1)? Is cosine similarity using the inner product of the embedding vectors?

3.	It is unclear how does the method is robust to imbalanced datasets? What is the appropriate $\omega$ in this case? 

4.	It may not be completely correct to claim that the method does not need the number of clusters to be known. To complete the clustering task, finally you may have to rely on a clustering algorithm to the learned spherical representations. 

5.	The clusters are equidistant in angular space which may be improved by considering class specific distance based on cluster similarities.

6.	Figure 6 does not strongly show the strength of the SpherePair regularization as the performance in lambda=0 is more or less the same as nonzero lambda in many settings

7.	It is mentioned that one of the limitations of the approach is that it does not support incomplete annotations. So, what is C (the number of pairwise constraints) in your settings? Does your analysis also consider fully observed constraints?

8.	The notation C is not explicitly defined.

LIMITATIONS:
yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
DECISION:
Accept (poster)

COMMENT:
This paper proposes a deep constrained clustering (DCC) method that incorporates an angular loss called SpherePair loss with a geometric formulation to effectively learn cluster separation and improve performance in downstream clusterings tasks. The authors show comparative evaluations on multiple benchmarks to demonstrate the efficacy of the method.

Overall, the paper is written well and the authors provide good theoretical justification coupled with experiments on multiple datasets & ablation studies to tease apart the contribution of the method under various settings. One of the weaknesses highlighted by reviewers is that the gains shown in the paper do not seem significant compared to other baselines. While the authors mention (in the rebuttal) that they would like to emphasize the performance improvement for “limited constraint and heavy imbalance” setting, however real-world datasets may not naturally conform to this scenario. Perhaps there is a different way to highlight the efficacy of the learned representation for some other downstream task — would help strengthen the empirical part of the claim made in the paper. The paper reports evaluation results on multiple datasets which is good, one suggestion is to include comparisons on additional large(r) benchmark datasets — will demonstrate the utility of the approach for real-world settings. Otherwise, this is a well-rounded paper. The authors are encouraged to incorporate all the feedback including several clarifications noted in the discussions (e.g., no requirement for exact #clusters) to further improve the paper quality.
reviews.TITLE: Decision
